title: Best Large Parrot Cages 2026: Spacious Homes for Macaws, Cockatoos & African Greys
description: A macaw's beak can snap the welds on a cheap cage in minutes. A cockatoo needs room to spread 3-foot wings. An African Grey is smart enough to dismantle anything flimsy. Large parrots demand cages that are spacious, indestructible, and intelligently designed — and this guide covers the 7 best cages for big birds in 2026.
content:
Why a Large Parrot Cage Is the Most Important Investment You'll Make for Your Bird

There's a saying among veteran parrot keepers: "Buy the biggest cage you can afford — and then buy one size bigger." Large parrots — macaws, cockatoos, African Greys, Amazons, and Eclectus — are among the most intelligent, long-lived, and emotionally complex pets on Earth. A macaw can live 60-80 years, outliving its original owner. A cockatoo can form bonds as deep and complex as a human child. An African Grey has the cognitive abilities of a 5-year-old human and the emotional sensitivity to match. You don't put a being like that in a $150 wire box from a big-box pet store. You build them a home.

The cage is where your parrot will spend 8-12 hours per day (sleeping) plus whatever hours you're at work — for many companion parrots, that's 14-18 hours daily inside the cage. In the wild, a macaw flies 15-30 miles per day across rainforest canopies. Your captive macaw can't fly free, but it can — and must — climb, flap, hang, forage, and stretch inside its cage. A cage that's too small is not just uncomfortable — it's psychologically damaging. Under-stimulated, cramped parrots develop stereotypies: repetitive, purposeless behaviors like pacing, head-bobbing, feather-plucking, and self-mutilation. These behaviors, once established, are extraordinarily difficult to reverse. The most common root cause is insufficient cage space.

A proper large parrot cage is fundamentally different from the cages you see for budgies, cockatiels, or conures. The bar spacing must be 3/4 inch to 1-1/2 inches — just wide enough for the bird to climb comfortably, just narrow enough that the bird can't squeeze its head through and get stuck or injured. The bar gauge must be at least 4-5mm for medium-large parrots (Grey, Amazon) and 5-6mm for extra-large parrots (macaw, large cockatoo) — anything thinner will be bent, snapped, or chewed through by a determined beak. The cage construction must use stainless steel or powder-coated wrought iron — painted cages will be stripped by an African Grey in a matter of days, and the ingested paint chips can cause heavy metal poisoning. The welds must be rock-solid; a blue-and-gold macaw can exert over 500 PSI of bite pressure, enough to snap poor welds that would hold a smaller parrot indefinitely.

The minimum cage dimensions for large parrots are larger than most people realize. For a macaw or large cockatoo (Moluccan, Umbrella): minimum 48" wide x 36" deep x 60" tall (interior), with a preference for 60" x 48" x 72" whenever space permits. For an African Grey, Amazon, or Eclectus: minimum 36" wide x 24" deep x 48" tall, preferably 48" x 30" x 60". These dimensions allow the bird to fully extend both wings without touching the cage walls — a basic welfare requirement that far too many cages fail. When a parrot can't fully extend its wings, it can't perform the stretch-and-flap exercise behavior that maintains pectoral muscle mass and shoulder joint health. The result over years is muscle atrophy, joint stiffness, and a bird that can't fly even if given the opportunity.

This guide covers the 7 best large parrot cages for 2026, spanning budget-friendly entry-level options for African Greys and Amazons to premium stainless steel mansions for macaws that will outlive their owners. We'll cover what to look for in bar spacing, bar gauge, materials, door design, tray systems, casters, and assembly — because when you're dealing with a bird that can undo nuts and bolts with its tongue, every design detail matters.

What to Look For in a Large Parrot Cage: The Critical Factors

  • Bar Spacing: This is the most critical spec — get it wrong and your bird is either stuck or escaped. For macaws and large cockatoos: 1" to 1-1/2" bar spacing. For African Greys, Amazons, and Eclectus: 3/4" to 1" spacing. For smaller large parrots (Senegals, caiques, small conures): 5/8" to 3/4" spacing. A bar gap that's too wide is a strangulation hazard — the bird gets its head through but can't pull it back, and the panic response can cause cervical injury or death. A bar gap that's too narrow makes climbing difficult and traps toes. Always verify the bar spacing spec from the manufacturer — don't rely on product photos or Amazon descriptions, which can be inaccurate
  • Bar Gauge (Thickness): For macaws and large cockatoos: minimum 5mm bar gauge, preferably 6mm (solid stainless steel rods). For African Greys and Amazons: minimum 4mm. A macaw's beak can snap a 3mm wire in a single bite — and once one bar is broken, the bird has an escape route. Powder-coated bars are standard for mid-range cages; stainless steel bars are premium. Avoid cages with bars described as "wire" — these are typically too thin and will bend under the weight of a climbing large parrot. Test the bars by squeezing them between your thumb and forefinger — on a macaw-appropriate cage, you shouldn't be able to flex the bars at all
  • Materials: Stainless steel is the gold standard — it's non-toxic (no coating to chip or ingest), rust-proof, easy to sanitize (can be pressure-washed), and essentially indestructible. Stainless cages cost 3-5x more than powder-coated but last 30+ years — over the lifespan of a macaw, the stainless cage is actually cheaper than buying 3-4 powder-coated cages. Powder-coated wrought iron is the standard for mid-range — the powder coat must be zinc-free and lead-free, specifically rated as "bird-safe" or "non-toxic." Substandard powder coating will chip under a large parrot's beak, and the exposed iron underneath will rust. Avoid: painted cages (the paint will be stripped and ingested), galvanized wire (zinc is toxic to parrots — causes heavy metal poisoning), and anything described as "coated wire" that doesn't specify the coating material
  • Door Design: Large parrots figure out simple latch mechanisms — a cockatoo can open a slide-bolt latch with its beak and tongue in under 30 seconds. The cage door needs a secondary locking mechanism that a bird cannot physically reach from inside the cage: a padlock, a carabiner clip, or a two-step latch that requires a pulling motion (which parrots can do) plus a twisting motion (which they cannot). The main door should be large enough that you can comfortably reach every corner of the cage interior — a 24" x 36" door opening on a 48"-wide cage is standard. Multiple feeder doors (smaller doors at perch height for food and water dish access) reduce the number of times you need to open the main door, which reduces escape opportunities
  • Tray and Grate System: The cage should have a removable bottom grate (where the bird stands) and a removable tray (below the grate, catching droppings and debris). The grate keeps the bird off the tray (preventing contact with droppings and spoiled food) and the tray slides out for daily cleaning. Look for a tray with a raised lip — it prevents debris from being kicked out when your parrot flaps its wings. The best trays are stainless steel or heavy-duty plastic with a smooth, non-porous surface that can be wiped clean. Metal grate bars should be spaced close enough (1/2" to 3/4") that the bird's foot can't slip through and trap a leg — grate entrapment is a common injury in cages with widely spaced grate bars
  • Seed Guards / Skirt: Large parrots are champion mess-makers. A macaw can fling food debris 4-6 feet in every direction. A seed guard — a metal or acrylic skirt that extends outward from the cage's lower perimeter — catches falling debris before it hits the floor. The skirt should extend at least 3-4 inches from the cage walls on all sides, and it should be removable for cleaning. Metal skirts are more durable but add weight. Acrylic skirts are lighter and look cleaner but can crack if the bird drops a heavy toy on them. Some high-end cages (King's Cages, Avian Adventures) include built-in seed guards; for others, you'll need to buy a separate skirt or use a cage mat underneath
  • Mobility and Casters: A 48" x 36" x 72" cage weighs 150-250 pounds empty — significantly more with perches, toys, and food dishes. You need to be able to move it for cleaning underneath and around it. Heavy-duty locking casters (4 wheels, all locking, rated for 500+ lbs) are non-negotiable. The casters should be 2-3 inches in diameter — small wheels catch on carpet and thresholds. Ball-bearing casters roll smoothly; plastic bushing casters bind and wear out. The cage stand should have a U-channel steel base (not thin tubular legs) designed to distribute the cage weight across the casters evenly. Never use a cage with removable casters that sit in sockets — an active macaw can rock the cage enough to pop a caster out of its socket

Top 7 Large Parrot Cages for 2026

The best large parrot cages for 2026

1. King's Cages 506 — Best Overall Large Parrot Cage for Macaws and Cockatoos

King's Cages has been the benchmark for large parrot cages in North America for over 30 years, and the Model 506 — also known as the Grand Macaw Mansion — is their flagship. The interior dimensions are 48" wide x 36" deep x 72" tall (exterior approximately 50" x 38" x 78" including the stand), providing 96 cubic feet of interior space for a macaw or large cockatoo to climb, flap, and explore. The cage is built from 5mm powder-coated wrought iron bars with 1" spacing — the sweet spot for macaw safety: wide enough for comfortable climbing, narrow enough that even an Umbrella cockatoo can't get its head through. The powder coating is a proprietary zinc-free, lead-free formula specifically tested for parrot safety. The cage arrives on a heavy-duty steel stand with four 3-inch locking casters that roll smoothly on hardwood, tile, and low-pile carpet.

The door system on the King's 506 is the best in the industry. The main door is 23" wide x 42" tall — large enough to step inside for cleaning — and uses a three-point locking system: a slide bolt, a twist-lock latch, and a carabiner clip hole for a padlock. A large macaw can manipulate a slide bolt with its tongue in under 60 seconds; it cannot operate a twist-lock or a carabiner. The three feeder doors (one front, two sides) are positioned at perch height and use the same secure latch design. The dome top opens flat to create a play-top area (great for the bird to perch on during supervised out-of-cage time), and the dome bars are reinforced with cross-bracing — unlike budget dome-top cages where the dome is structurally weak and bends if the bird hangs from it.

The tray system is equally well-designed. The bottom grate is 3/8" bar spacing (safe for large parrot feet) with a center support bar that prevents sagging under a 3-pound macaw's weight. The tray below is heavy-gauge stainless steel with a 1-inch raised lip — debris stays contained, and the smooth surface wipes clean in seconds. The seed guard is a 3-inch metal skirt that extends on all four sides, catching approximately 70% of flung food and feather debris. The cage includes two stainless steel feeder bowls (30 oz, heavy enough that a macaw can't flip them), two natural wood perches (manzanita, 1.5" diameter), and all assembly hardware. The cage requires two people to assemble — plan on 2-3 hours — and the instructions are clear photo-based diagrams. At $650-800, the King's 506 is a serious investment, but for a bird that will live 60+ years, it's the right cage.

Pros:

  • 48" x 36" x 72" interior — 96 cubic feet, full wing extension for even the largest macaws and cockatoos
  • 5mm powder-coated wrought iron bars, 1" spacing — macaw-proof construction, safe bar gap
  • Triple-locking door system — slide bolt + twist-lock + padlock point, escape-proof for even the smartest birds
  • Stainless steel tray with raised lip — easy daily cleaning, debris containment
  • Dome-top play area — additional supervised out-of-cage space, reinforced for climbing
  • 3-inch locking casters on heavy-duty stand — smooth rolling on all surfaces
  • 3 feeder doors with secure latches — reduces main door openings, less escape opportunity
  • Includes stainless bowls, natural wood perches, and seed guard — ready to use out of the box
  • 30+ year company track record — parts and replacement hardware available for every model

Cons:

  • $650-800 — premium priced, the most expensive powder-coated cage in this guide
  • 250+ lbs assembled weight — requires two people to assemble and move
  • Assembly is a 2-3 hour project — 60+ bolts, needs a power drill with hex bit (manual allen wrench included but slow)
  • Powder coating (not stainless steel) — will eventually chip after 10+ years of macaw abuse, requires touch-up
  • Dome-top bars collect dust — harder to clean than flat-top cages
  • Cage is too large to fit through standard 30" doorways assembled — will need to be partially disassembled to move rooms

Rating: 5/5 | Best For: Macaws, large cockatoos (Moluccan, Umbrella), multi-large-bird households, lifetime cage

2. A&E Cage Co. Extra Large Dome Top — Best Value Large Parrot Cage

A&E Cage Co. has been King's Cages' primary competitor for decades, and their Extra Large Dome Top (model 4032FL or 4824FL depending on retailer) delivers 80% of the King's 506 quality for roughly 60% of the price. The interior dimensions are 40" wide x 30" deep x 62" tall (the 4032FL) — slightly smaller than the King's 506 but still adequate for a single macaw or cockatoo that gets daily out-of-cage exercise. The bars are 4.5mm powder-coated wrought iron with 1" spacing — slightly thinner than King's (4.5mm vs 5mm), which matters for the largest macaws (Blue-and-Gold, Greenwing) but is adequate for African Greys, Amazons, and smaller cockatoos (Goffin's, Rose-breasted). The powder coating is bird-safe, zinc/lead-free, and well-applied with no visible drips or thin spots.

The A&E's stand-out feature is the play-top with a removable tray — the dome top has a perch and a sliding tray underneath that catches droppings during play-top time, preventing the bird from soiling the cage interior below. This is a genuinely useful feature for birds that spend supervised time on top of their cage. The main door is 20" x 36" with a two-point slide-bolt latch (adequate for Greys and Amazons, but we'd add a carabiner for macaws). The feeder doors are deep-set — 6 inches from the cage exterior to the bowl holder — preventing the bird from reaching out and throwing bowls. The seed guard is a 2.5-inch acrylic skirt (not metal — lighter but less durable than King's metal skirt). At $400-500, the A&E Extra Large Dome Top is the best value large parrot cage on the market. If the King's 506 is out of budget, this is the next best thing.

Pros:

  • 40" x 30" x 62" interior — adequate for single macaws and cockatoos with daily out-of-cage exercise
  • Play-top with removable tray — convenient supervised out-of-cage time without soiling the interior
  • $400-500 — 40% cheaper than the King's 506, excellent value for Greys and Amazons
  • 4.5mm powder-coated bars with 1" spacing — adequate for most large parrots
  • Deep-set feeder doors — prevents bowl-throwing, a common large parrot behavior
  • Includes 2 stainless bowls, 2 wood perches, and acrylic seed guard
  • Available at major retailers — Petco, Amazon, Chewy, easier to buy than King's (which is dealer-only)

Cons:

  • 4.5mm bars — might not survive a determined Blue-and-Gold macaw over years, consider 5mm+ for largest species
  • Slide-bolt latches (no twist-lock) — macaws can learn these, you'll need to add a carabiner or padlock
  • Acrylic seed guard (not metal) — lighter duty, can crack if heavy toys fall on it
  • Assembly hardware is lower quality — bolts are zinc-plated (not stainless), can rust in humid environments
  • 40" width is borderline for a macaw — wing extension may touch bars, especially for Hyacinth macaws
  • Caster quality is mediocre — small 2-inch wheels that bind on carpet, upgrade to aftermarket casters recommended

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: African Greys, Amazons, Eclectus, small cockatoos, budget macaw cage with daily out-of-cage time

3. Avian Adventures Stainless Steel Loro — Best Premium Stainless Steel Cage

Large Parrot Cage Setup Guide: Creating an Enriching Environment

How to properly set up and furnish a large parrot cage

  1. Perch Placement Strategy: Install at least 3-4 perches at different heights and orientations — never place perches directly above food and water bowls (droppings contamination). The highest perch should be at roughly 2/3 of the cage height — birds naturally seek the highest point, and placing perches all the way at the top causes them to hang from the ceiling bars, which is uncomfortable and can injure feet. Use natural wood perches of varying diameters (3/4" to 1-1/2" for large parrots) — the varying diameter exercises foot muscles and prevents pressure sores that develop from uniform dowel perches. Manzanita, dragonwood, and java wood are excellent. Include one rough-textured concrete or sand perch for natural nail and beak conditioning, but place it where the bird doesn't sleep (rough-textured perches cause foot irritation with prolonged contact)
  2. Toy Rotation System: Large parrots destroy toys rapidly — a macaw can demolish a $30 wooden toy in 2-3 days. Maintain a collection of 8-12 toys and rotate 3-4 into the cage per week, removing the previous set. This prevents toy boredom (a bored parrot is a feather-plucking parrot) and extends toy life. Include different toy types in each rotation: a foraging toy (treats hidden inside destructible material), a chew toy (soft wood blocks, palm leaf, cardboard), a puzzle toy (mechanical challenge with a food reward), and a foot toy (a small toy the bird can hold and manipulate). Never use toys with small parts that can be swallowed, bells with clappers that can be removed, or chain links that toes can catch in
  3. Food and Water Station: Use heavy stainless steel bowls (20-30 oz for large parrots) that can't be flipped — bowls should lock into holders on the cage bars, not sit loose on the floor. Position water bowls away from food bowls and perches to minimize contamination. Provide a separate bathing dish for birds that enjoy splashing (or use a cage-mountable shower perch for bathroom time). Never use water bottles with ball-bearing sippers for parrots — they can't get enough water volume from these and may dehydrate, and the ball mechanism can trap a beak. Change water twice daily — large parrots are champion soup-makers, dunking food in their water
  4. Substrate Management: Use cage liner paper or newspaper (black-and-white print only, no color ads — colored inks may contain toxic compounds) on the tray below the grate. Change paper daily — droppings accumulate fast with a large parrot. Avoid: corn cob bedding (grows mold and Aspergillus — fatal to parrots), walnut shell litter (dust causes respiratory issues), cedar or pine shavings (aromatic oils are toxic to avian respiratory systems), and cat litter (clumping or otherwise — ingestion risk). The safest option is plain, unprinted paper. For the floor area under and around the cage, a washable cage mat or vinyl floor protector saves your flooring from flung food and feather dander
  5. Lighting and Placement: Position the cage where the bird can see household activity (parrots are flock animals and need social contact) but not in direct line of kitchen fumes (Teflon/PTFE cookware off-gassing at high temperatures is deadly to birds — even in a different room, fumes travel through ventilation). Place the cage against at least one wall for security (birds feel exposed in open-center room positions). Provide a full-spectrum UVB/A bird lamp positioned 12-18 inches above the highest perch, on a timer for 10-12 hours of daylight. Avoid direct window placement — glass magnifies heat (can overheat the bird) and drafts cause respiratory stress

FAQ

Can I keep two macaws in one cage?

It depends entirely on the individual birds. Bonded pairs (birds that have lived together for years, mutually preen, and show no aggression) can share a cage if it's large enough — minimum 60" x 48" for two macaws. Unbonded birds of the same size in a cage together will almost certainly fight, and macaw fights are brutal — severed toes, broken beaks, and deaths occur regularly. Never "try it and see what happens" — introduce birds in neutral territory over weeks, watch for body language, and only co-house if they're clearly bonded. Even bonded pairs need their own food and water stations — competition over resources causes fights. A double-wide cage with a divider (like the Centurion) is safer than a single open cage for two birds.

Why is my parrot plucking its feathers? Is the cage too small?

Feather plucking has multiple potential causes, and inadequate cage space is a common one — but not the only one. A cage that's too small causes chronic stress, which can trigger plucking. But other causes include: malnutrition (vitamin A deficiency is a classic plucking trigger), hormonal imbalances (particularly in mature cockatoos), boredom and lack of foraging opportunities, insufficient sleep (parrots need 10-12 hours of darkness daily), skin parasites (mites), and underlying disease (liver, kidney, or respiratory). A larger cage is a good first step, but you need an avian veterinarian to run blood work and rule out medical causes. Behavioral plucking — once a bird has established the habit — is extremely difficult to reverse, and prevention (by providing adequate space and enrichment from day one) is vastly more effective than treatment.

Is a used cage safe? What should I check for?

Used cages can be excellent value — large parrot cages depreciate heavily on the secondhand market — but you must inspect for: (1) Rust — any rust on the bars, tray, or hardware, particularly in corners and weld points. Rust indicates that moisture has degraded the protective coating and the underlying iron is oxidizing. Rust patches are sharp (can cut feet and tongues) and the iron oxide can be toxic if consumed. Light surface rust on a tray is manageable; rust on the cage bars means the cage is at end of life. (2) Broken welds — inspect every bar junction. A single broken weld creates a flex point that the bird will find and exploit. Macaws can break additional welds once one has failed. (3) Chipped coating — powder coat chips near the bird's favorite perching spots (where they chew the bars). Chipped areas expose bare metal that will rust and that the bird can ingest. (4) Previous disease — thoroughly disinfect with a veterinary-grade cleaner (F10 SC or dilute bleach 1:10, thoroughly rinsed and fully dried for 24 hours before use). (5) Missing or damaged hardware — check that all casters lock, all feeder doors latch securely, and all bolts are present and tight.

Should I cover my parrot's cage at night?

Yes — for most large parrots, a cage cover provides the darkness and security needed for proper sleep. Parrots need 10-12 hours of uninterrupted darkness per night for hormone regulation, immune function, and behavioral health. A cage cover blocks ambient light (street lights, electronics, early sunrise) and reduces drafts. Use a breathable fabric (cotton, not polyester or nylon — birds can overheat under non-breathable covers). The cover should not touch the cage bars (brids may pull fabric through the bars and ingest threads). Never cover the cage during the day as a "punishment" for screaming — this creates negative associations with the cover and turns it into a stressor, not a comfort. Some parrots (particularly cockatoos) may be frightened by covers — if your bird shows distress (pacing, alarm calls) when covered, use a room-darkening solution instead (blackout curtains, moving the cage to a dark room at night).

Your parrot's cage is not just a container. It's where your bird eats, sleeps, plays, and spends the majority of its hours for decades. Buy the biggest, strongest cage you can afford.
